diagnosis: Chronic fatigue syndrome (myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome)
icd_category: R53.82 — Chronic fatigue, unspecified / G93.32 — Myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome
one_liner: A 36-year-old woman presents with 9 months of debilitating fatigue, post-exertional malaise, unrefreshing sleep, and cognitive difficulties following a viral illness, with extensive normal laboratory workup.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient reports 9 months of profound, debilitating fatigue that began abruptly following a viral illness (confirmed influenza). Prior to the illness, she was highly functional — running half-marathons, working full-time as a project manager, and socially active. Since then, she has been unable to return to her pre-illness activity level. Her fatigue is not relieved by rest; she sleeps 9-10 hours per night but wakes feeling 'like she didn't sleep at all' (unrefreshing sleep). The hallmark symptom is post-exertional malaise (PEM): any physical or cognitive exertion beyond minimal activity causes a severe worsening of symptoms that lasts 24-72 hours. For example, walking to the grocery store triggers 2 days of bed-bound exhaustion with worsened body aches and difficulty thinking. She describes cognitive difficulties — 'brain fog,' difficulty finding words, poor short-term memory, and inability to multitask. She has diffuse myalgias and arthralgias without swelling. She has orthostatic symptoms (lightheadedness upon standing). She has reduced her work to part-time and has stopped all exercise. She denies depressed mood, stating 'I'm not depressed — I'm frustrated because I want to do things but my body won't let me.'
stage2.pmh: No significant prior medical or psychiatric history. Previously highly active and functional.
stage2.medications: Melatonin 3 mg at bedtime, ibuprofen PRN for body aches
stage2.family_history: Mother with fibromyalgia. Father with hypertension. Sister with irritable bowel syndrome. No family history of autoimmune or neurological disease.
stage2.social_history: Previously worked full-time as a project manager, now part-time. Single, lives alone. Previously ran half-marathons, now cannot walk more than 2 blocks. Non-smoker. Rarely drinks alcohol (cannot tolerate it since illness). No illicit drug use. College-educated. Social life has diminished due to inability to participate in activities.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Well-appearing woman who looks her stated age but appears fatigued and speaks slowly. HEENT: Mildly dry mucous membranes. Cardiovascular: Mild resting tachycardia, regular rhythm, no murmurs; Orthostatic tachycardia demonstrated. Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no hepatosplenomegaly. Musculoskeletal: Diffuse tender points but no joint swelling, warmth, or erythema; Full range of motion. Skin: Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII intact; Motor strength 5/5 throughout but reports effort-related exhaustion (symptoms worsen during sustained testing); Sensation intact; Reflexes 2+ symmetric; Gait normal at baseline but patient reports significant fatigue after walking 50 feet. Mental Status: Alert, oriented, cooperative; Mood 'frustrated; ' Affect congruent; Thought process linear; Cognition — slow word-finding during conversation, asks to repeat questions.

lab_results.result: Na 139, K 4.1, Cl 102, CO2 24, BUN 12, Cr 0.7, Glucose 88, Ca 9.4, AST 20, ALT 18, Alk Phos 55, Albumin 4.0
lab_results.clinical_significance: Normal metabolic panel; no organ dysfunction

imaging_results.result: No infiltrates, masses, or lymphadenopathy. Normal cardiac silhouette.

key_tests: Comprehensive medical history — identifying cardinal ME/CFS features: post-exertional malaise, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive impairment, and orthostatic intolerance
Orthostatic vital signs — demonstrating orthostatic tachycardia (HR increase >30 bpm) consistent with POTS-like autonomic dysfunction
Extensive normal laboratory panel — ruling out thyroid disease, anemia, autoimmune disease, adrenal insufficiency, infections, and metabolic derangements
PHQ-9 — score 5 (minimal depression), supporting that fatigue is not primarily depression-driven
acceptable_tests: CBC, CMP, TSH — standard fatigue workup
Lyme antibody — screening for tick-borne illness
low_yield_tests: EBV serologies (past EBV infection is ubiquitous; active EBV reactivation does not explain chronic symptoms)
Extensive autoimmune panels beyond ANA (low pre-test probability without specific clinical features)

stage5.final_clue: Additionally, she has orthostatic intolerance (orthostatic tachycardia). The onset was post-infectious (influenza).

debrief.classic_presentation: A previously highly functional adult who develops debilitating fatigue following a viral illness, with the cardinal features: (1) substantial reduction in activity that does not resolve with rest, (2) post-exertional malaise (PEM — the hallmark symptom, where physical or cognitive exertion triggers disproportionate and prolonged symptom worsening lasting >24 hours), (3) unrefreshing sleep, and either (4a) cognitive impairment ('brain fog') or (4b) orthostatic intolerance. The condition is often post-infectious (EBV, influenza, COVID-19) and affects predominantly women aged 30-50.
debrief.attending_mode_notes: ME/CFS is a legitimate, disabling neuroimmune condition — not a psychological disorder. The 2015 IOM report renamed it Systemic Exertion Intolerance Disease (SEID) and established evidence-based diagnostic criteria, replacing the older Fukuda criteria. Key clinical challenges: (1) Post-exertional malaise (PEM) is the DEFINING feature — if PEM is absent, the diagnosis should be reconsidered. PEM distinguishes ME/CFS from depression, fibromyalgia, and deconditioning. (2) Graded exercise therapy (GET) is CONTRAINDICATED — unlike depression or deconditioning where exercise improves function, in ME/CFS exercise worsens symptoms (PEM). The NICE 2021 guidelines removed GET from recommendations. Pacing (activity management within the 'energy envelope') is the approach. (3) Orthostatic intolerance (POTS-like features) is present in ~70% of ME/CFS patients — tilt table testing or active standing test can demonstrate this. (4) The overlap with fibromyalgia is ~30-50% but they are distinct conditions. (5) COVID-19 has dramatically increased awareness of ME/CFS, as many Long COVID patients meet ME/CFS criteria. (6) There is no FDA-approved treatment — management is symptom-based, and the prognosis is guarded (only ~5% fully recover). Disability accommodations may be necessary.
debrief.pathophysiology: The pathophysiology of ME/CFS is incompletely understood but involves multiple interconnected systems: (1) Immune dysregulation — impaired NK cell function, altered cytokine profiles (elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines), possible autoimmune component. (2) Energy metabolism — mitochondrial dysfunction with impaired oxidative phosphorylation, demonstrated by two-day CPET showing inability to reproduce day-1 exercise capacity on day 2. (3) Autonomic nervous system dysfunction — reduced cerebral blood flow, orthostatic intolerance, dysregulation of the sympathetic/parasympathetic balance. (4) Neuroinflammation — PET studies show microglial activation in the brain. (5) Microbiome alterations — gut dysbiosis with decreased diversity. (6) Epigenetic changes — altered DNA methylation patterns. The post-infectious trigger suggests a hit-and-run mechanism where acute infection triggers a persistent immunological and metabolic derangement in genetically susceptible individuals.
debrief.treatment_overview: No curative treatment exists — management is symptom-based. Activity management/pacing: help the patient identify their 'energy envelope' (the level of activity they can sustain without triggering PEM) and stay within it. REST when PEM occurs. Sleep optimization: sleep hygiene, low-dose trazodone (25-50 mg) or suvorexant for unrefreshing sleep; melatonin for circadian issues. Orthostatic intolerance: increased salt and fluid intake (2-3 L daily), compression stockings, fludrocortisone or midodrine if POTS-like symptoms are severe. Cognitive symptoms: neurocognitive accommodations, avoid cognitive overexertion. Pain management: low-dose naltrexone (1.5-4.5 mg) has emerging evidence; gabapentin or duloxetine for comorbid pain. Address comorbidities: IBS symptoms (low-FODMAP diet), fibromyalgia overlap (pregabalin). Psychosocial support: validation, disability advocacy, CBT (for coping, NOT as curative treatment). Avoid: GET (worsens PEM), stimulants (crash-and-boom pattern).
high_yield_pearls: Post-exertional malaise (PEM) is the HALLMARK of ME/CFS — if absent, reconsider the diagnosis. PEM is disproportionate worsening of all symptoms following physical or cognitive exertion, lasting >24 hours.
Graded exercise therapy (GET) is CONTRAINDICATED in ME/CFS — unlike depression or deconditioning, exercise worsens the condition. NICE 2021 guidelines removed GET from recommendations.
ME/CFS is NOT primarily a psychiatric disorder — it is a neuroimmune condition with demonstrated abnormalities in immune function, energy metabolism, and autonomic regulation.
The 2015 IOM diagnostic criteria require all three core symptoms (fatigue with substantial activity reduction >6 months, PEM, unrefreshing sleep) PLUS cognitive impairment OR orthostatic intolerance.
COVID-19 is now the most common infectious trigger of new ME/CFS cases — approximately 50% of Long COVID patients meet ME/CFS diagnostic criteria.
Only ~5% of ME/CFS patients fully recover — honest prognosis counseling and disability accommodations are important components of care.
sources: Institute of Medicine (IOM/NASEM). Beyond Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome: Redefining an Illness, 2015 — https://www.uptodate.com/contents/search?search=Institute+of+Medicine+%28IOM%2FNASEM%29.+Beyond+Myalgic+Encephalomyelitis%2FChronic+Fatigue+Syndrome%3A+Redefining+an+Illness%2C+2015
NICE Guideline: Myalgic encephalomyelitis (or encephalopathy)/chronic fatigue syndrome: diagnosis and management (NG206), 2021 — https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance
Bateman L et al. ME/CFS: Clinician Coalition Treatment Recommendations, 2021 — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=Bateman+L+et+al.+ME%2FCFS%3A+Clinician+Coalition+Treatment+Recommendations%2C+2021

Komaroff AL, Lipkin WI. ME/CFS and Long COVID share underlying pathophysiology. Front Med, 2023 — https://www.uptodate.com/contents/search?search=Komaroff+AL%2C+Lipkin+WI.+ME%2FCFS+and+Long+COVID+share+underlying+pathophysiology.+Front+Med%2C+2023

Rowe PC et al. Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome Diagnosis and Management in Young People: A Primer. Front Pediatr, 2017 — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=Rowe+PC+et+al.+Myalgic+Encephalomyelitis%2FChronic+Fatigue+Syndrome+Diagnosis+and+Management+in+Young+People%3A+A+Primer.+Front+Pediatr%2C+2017

after_stage2_more_likely: ME/CFS — post-infectious onset, post-exertional malaise (hallmark), unrefreshing sleep, cognitive impairment, orthostatic tachycardia, debilitating course from previously high-functioning baseline
Fibromyalgia — diffuse tender points and myalgias, but PEM and post-infectious onset are more characteristic of ME/CFS
after_stage2_less_likely: Major depression — patient denies depressed mood, expresses frustration and desire to be active (ego-dystonic), no anhedonia beyond physical limitation
Hypothyroidism — no cold intolerance, constipation, or thyromegaly
Adrenal insufficiency — no hyperpigmentation, no hypotension (orthostatic tachycardia without hypotension is more consistent with POTS/ME/CFS)
after_labs_top3: Myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome — meets all IOM diagnostic criteria, exhaustive normal medical workup, post-infectious onset, cardinal PEM symptom
Fibromyalgia (comorbid) — diffuse tender points present but PEM and orthostatic intolerance distinguish primary ME/CFS
Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (comorbid/overlapping) — orthostatic tachycardia demonstrated; POTS is present in ~70% of ME/CFS patients
